title: Theory of learning of high-dimensional controlled non-linear dynamical systems (I): models and methods
abstract: Neural ordinary differential equations (neural ODEs) have rapidly gained prominence as a powerful and unifying framework for conceptualizing artificial neural networks, elegantly connecting the continuous-time modeling of dynamical systems with the discrete, data-driven paradigm of modern deep learning. Beyond their practical advantages they offer fresh theoretical insights into the training and generalization properties of neural networks. The distinctive feature of this framework is its dual dynamical nature: inference dynamics, which govern the ODE evolution during forward computation, and training dynamics, which control the optimization of model parameters. This makes neural ODEs a particularly well-suited theoretical framework for studying a large variety of settings such as multi-layer neural networks (ResNets for example), autoregressive models (with next-token generation dynamics), generative models, and recurrent neural networks in theoretical neuroscience. In this work, we introduce a theoretically grounded class of models for studying neural ODEs trained via online stochastic gradient descent. We solve the training dynamics of these models via dynamical mean field theory and derive learning curves in the high-dimensional limit.
summary: This paper applies advanced tools from statistical physics, particularly Dynamical Mean Field Theory, to model the learning process in Neural ODEs. It treats the training dynamics as a coupled, high-dimensional stochastic control problem between a 'teacher' and a 'student' system. The work provides theoretical predictions for how deep neural networks learn by analyzing the evolution of correlation functions in the infinite-dimensional limit.
main_problem: The work aims to theoretically understand feature learning dynamics within high-dimensional, non-linear dynamical systems trained using online stochastic gradient descent.
main_result: The authors develop a framework using Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) to derive self-consistent equations governing the evolution of summary statistics for both inference and training processes in the high-dimensional limit.
method: The core methodology involves recasting the learning problem into an optimal control setting, applying path integral techniques, and solving the resulting coupled dynamics using Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT).
model_or_system: The model centers on Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) within a teacher-student framework. The system involves coupled stochastic processes for state variables ($x$, $y$) and adjoint fields ($\pi$), governed by non-linear dynamics.
key_observables: Learning curves, correlation functions ($C_{oo'}$), response functions ($R_{oo'}$), and the training objective/loss function derived from these correlations.
important_parameters: The analysis is performed in the high-dimensional limit, considering parameters like the learning rate ($\eta$), noise levels ($ eta$), and time scales ($T_s, T_t$).
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include adopting a teacher-student setup, assuming noiseless dynamics for initial DMFT derivations (though noise is incorporated later), and relying on the high-dimensional limit to close the system via summary statistics.
figures_summary: Figures compare DMFT predictions against numerical simulations, showing how observables like $C_{	heta	heta}$ and $m(\alpha)$ approach unity as training progresses, indicating successful student alignment with the teacher.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the coupled dynamical system (teacher/student) to formulating the objective via a Lagrangian. It then applies path integrals and DMFT to derive self-consistent equations for summary statistics, culminating in predictions for learning curves across various complex regimes.
why_interesting: While focused on ML, the use of mean-field theory, renormalization group concepts (implied by high-dimensional limits), and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics techniques to analyze system evolution is highly relevant to AMO physics studying complex interacting many-body systems or open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
